transcript:
What you're watching is 500 tiny points breathing in and out of a single shape — spiky ball, blooming flower, scattering dust — over and over, never quite the same way twice.

Here's the thing: nothing in the code ever decides to make a flower. Look at the array called "who I am" — that's the 500 particles, each one just running "update one of me," checking neighbors for separation, alignment, cohesion. No particle knows it's part of a figure. The shape you're seeing is a side effect the code names directly — there's a variable literally called "grip strength" that cycles between cohesion and dispersal, and when four or more neighbors happen to align, a counter called "cohering" ticks up and the particle glows warmer — that's the coalition heat you see in the color shift. That warmth isn't a reward for the whole; it's a local, temporary bookkeeping trick that happens to look like a self holding together.

So the flower isn't hiding in the code waiting to bloom — it's genuinely improvised each cycle from blind local rules, then the same rules dissolve it. The coherence you're pattern-matching onto is entirely yours, not the particles'.
